text: <par>W.T. French (1983:455) assigned Phom <latinform>bəʔ</latinform> to PNN <reconstruction>*ʔ-bəw</reconstruction>; see <xref ref="1811">#1811</xref>.</par>
<par>But cf. p. 44 Chang (#77757): <latinform>ok</latinform> seems to be pfx? and <latinform>M2</latinform> carries the semantics?</par>
<par>Phom <latinform>di</latinform> (<reconstruction>*ji</reconstruction> expected). WTF-PNN < n.582, p.127. Also in GEM-CNL</par>
<par>Note the secondary palatalization of the vowel in Chang <latinform>yik</latinform>.</par>
<par>I tagged this based on its similarity to <latinform>a kʰo naŋ</latinform> ‘above’ (RN 78036)--Matt.</par>

<par>According to French, the first syllable means 'skin', so that the compound means literally "head-skin".  French has shown that Proto-Northern-Naga (PNN) <reconstruction>*kh</reconstruction> > Konyak and Phom <latinform>š</latinform>.</par>
<par>This form may be in error; perhaps <latinform>phe-lim</latinform>--WTF-PNN 522</par>

<par>cf. Wancho <latinform>cak diŋ</latinform> ‘wrist’ (‘hand’ + ??) and <latinform>ci diŋ</latinform> ‘ankle’ (‘leg’ + ??). Perhaps <latinform>diŋ</latinform> means ‘joint’ or ‘neck’ , and is allofamic to the first syllable of this form, din ku. Note also the similarity to the Komrem form <latinform>kədəŋ kʰul</latinform> ‘windpipe / trachea’ .</par>

<par>It looks superficially as though the last syllable of this Chang form goes with <xref ref="288">#288</xref>, but since <latinform>-laŋ</latinform> appears as a formative in several rounded bodyparts, we assign this syllable to <xref ref="2045">#2045</xref>, following French 1983: 540.</par>

<par>The second syllable of this Chang form is referred by W. T. French (1983: 540) to PNN <reconstruction>*C-ruŋ</reconstruction> 'round (of body parts)'. This binome is thus not a dimidiation of a sesquisyllabic combination of prefixal <reconstruction>*t-</reconstruction> plus root-initial <reconstruction>*l-</reconstruction> (as in Lepcha). Besides no final <latinform>-ŋ</latinform> occurs anywhere else in the set.</par>
<par>W.T. French (1983:449-50) sets up no fewer than four allofams of this root at the Proto-Northern Naga level.</par>
<par>Reflex was wrongly entered with [A] tone. Corrected to [B] tone based on original source (dwbruhn, 2011-11-16).</par>
